title: Recurrence reasoning

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Recurrence reasoning; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Algorithmic Foundations; August 2023; material applicable to Recurrence reasoning
sources.claim: A recurrence expresses work in terms of smaller inputs and nonrecursive cost, then expansion or a theorem derives an asymptotic bound.

sources.locator: CS2023 Mathematical and Statistical Foundations; August 2023; material applicable to Recurrence reasoning
sources.claim: A recurrence expresses work in terms of smaller inputs and nonrecursive cost, then expansion or a theorem derives an asymptotic bound.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A recurrence expresses work in terms of smaller inputs and nonrecursive cost, then expansion or a theorem derives an asymptotic bound.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: T(n)=2T(n/2)+O(n) yields O(n log n) for merge sort under its assumptions. Your goal is to derive and solve or bound simple decrease-by-one and divide-by-factor recurrences. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Readiness, algorithms and analysis; Array representation and operations; Call stack and resource cost; Time, space and asymptotic growth. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: A recurrence expresses work in terms of smaller inputs and nonrecursive cost, then expansion or a theorem derives an asymptotic bound. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: C and C++ expose finite native call stacks without guaranteed tail-call elimination; Java likewise has bounded stack frames; Python has a comparatively low recursion limit, so all four implementations need an iterative alternative when depth scales with input.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Master-theorem cases do not cover every recurrence, floors and uneven splits may need other arguments. The draft therefore rejects: Dropping the recursive term and keeping only local work gives the total complexity.

includes: Express recursive work as a recurrence and estimate common growth patterns.
Derive and solve or bound simple decrease-by-one and divide-by-factor recurrences.

C and C++ expose finite native call stacks without guaranteed tail-call elimination; Java likewise has bounded stack frames; Python has a comparatively low recursion limit, so all four implementations need an iterative alternative when depth scales with input.
excludes: Master-theorem cases do not cover every recurrence, floors and uneven splits may need other arguments.

outcomes: Derive and solve or bound simple decrease-by-one and divide-by-factor recurrences.

examples.title: Recurrence reasoning: language-neutral trace
examples.situation: T(n)=2T(n/2)+O(n) yields O(n log n) for merge sort under its assumptions.
walkthrough: Step 1 — State the abstract input and invariant: T(n)=2T(n/2)+O(n) yields O(n log n) for merge sort under its assumptions.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Include every recursive subproblem and combine level work across the recursion tree.
examples.result: The conceptual result follows because Include every recursive subproblem and combine level work across the recursion tree.

examples.title: Recurrence reasoning: implementation transfer
examples.situation: C and C++ expose finite native call stacks without guaranteed tail-call elimination; Java likewise has bounded stack frames; Python has a comparatively low recursion limit, so all four implementations need an iterative alternative when depth scales with input.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Keep the same specification and invariant across all four languages.

Step 2 — Map storage, library, equality, bounds, ownership, and error rules using: C and C++ expose finite native call stacks without guaranteed tail-call elimination; Java likewise has bounded stack frames; Python has a comparatively low recursion limit, so all four implementations need an iterative alternative when depth scales with input.
Step 3 — Retest this boundary in the selected implementation: Master-theorem cases do not cover every recurrence, floors and uneven splits may need other arguments.
examples.result: Concept mastery transfers, while implementation evidence remains language-specific.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Dropping the recursive term and keeping only local work gives the total complexity.
misconceptions.correction: Include every recursive subproblem and combine level work across the recursion tree.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use an invariant or counterexample to explain why this DSA belief fails: Dropping the recursive term and keeping only local work gives the total complexity.

usefulFor: Introducing structure, order, connectivity, or state transition before formal notation
limitations: A physical arrangement does not reproduce asymptotic cost, aliasing, numeric limits, recursion depth, or the four languages' implementation rules.
